hook: You already know how to model simple yes/no experiments with the binomial distribution — but what happens when events occur randomly over time, or when you're sampling without replacement from a finite group? Real-world counting problems come in many flavors that the binomial alone can't handle. In this unit, you'll build a powerful toolkit of discrete distributions that tackle exactly these richer, messier scenarios.
concept: Each distribution in this unit is a precision tool designed for a specific type of counting process — whether it's rare events, repeated trials until success, or draws from a fixed population. Together they form a family of *discrete probability models* unified by the idea that the right distributional assumption unlocks elegant, exact probability calculations.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to model the probability of a given number of rare or random events occurring over a fixed interval of time or space, using the parameter $\lambda$ to capture the average rate.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Moment Generating Functions for Discrete Distributions
topic_roadmap.description: Master the moment generating function $M_X(t) = E[e^{tX}]$ as a unified tool to efficiently derive means, variances, and identify distributions across the entire discrete family.
Apply the negative binomial distribution to calculate probabilities when you're counting the number of trials needed to achieve a fixed number of successes in repeated independent experiments.
You will learn to compute exact probabilities for sampling without replacement from a finite population, capturing the dependence between draws that the binomial model ignores.
